does: This paper presents RePair, a method for automated program repair using smaller language models (under 20B parameters) enhanced through process-based feedback and reinforcement learning. The authors construct CodeNet4Repair, a dataset containing multiple repair trajectories for buggy programs, and use it to fine-tune foundation models. Rather than requiring single-step fixes or explicit prompts, the approach enables models to receive iterative feedback from compilers and test cases, allowing them to learn repair policies that progressively modify code toward correct solutions. The method demonstrates that small-scale language models can achieve performance comparable to or exceeding much larger models when trained with proper process supervision.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research in neural automated program repair, which has evolved from syntax-based and template-based approaches toward large language model-based methods. It extends prior work on using compiler feedback and test-driven repair by incorporating reinforcement learning to optimize repair strategies, similar to approaches in math problem-solving and code generation. The paper responds to limitations identified in existing LM-based APR methods, which typically rely on few-shot prompting or require enormous model scales. It connects to broader research on process supervision in machine learning, where step-by-step feedback proves more effective than outcome-only supervision, as demonstrated in recent work on reasoning and code synthesis tasks.
enables: This work enables the deployment of effective automated program repair systems with significantly lower computational costs, making APR more accessible for real-world applications. By demonstrating that smaller models with proper training can compete with massive models, it opens pathways for on-device or resource-constrained repair systems. The reinforcement learning framework enables future work on adaptive repair policies that can learn from newly encountered bugs and improve over time. The CodeNet4Repair dataset and process supervision methodology provide infrastructure for further research into curriculum learning for repair, multi-step reasoning in code generation, and hybrid symbolic-neural approaches that leverage both compiler signals and learned repair heuristics for improved robustness in software systems.
